data.title: Politics And The Markets 03/10/25
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: Beware Of Following The Wall Street Fashion Show It's Too Late
data.description:
So what do I mean by the “Wall Street Fashion Show?”

I promise I didn’t make this notion up, unfortunately, there is now an actual “Wall Street Fashion Show,” so the Google search is loaded with pages and pages of PR on this show. I can't find a reference to the saying now. So take my word for it: "The Wall Street Fashion Show" is when an established narrative like AI gets long in the tooth, meaning that everyone already knows that AI is the big cheese. Along comes another shiny object, and the hot money wants that shiny thing. This is what is meant by “Fashion Show” this new thing becomes the fad of the moment. As I said, It often happens that the “old” investment thesis is fully known and fully appreciated. Certainly, everyone got the memo that the Magnificent 7 were, well, magnificent. All it took was a little negativity, and there was a rush to the door.

At the same time, the new shiny thing are the European and Chinese stocks. Perhaps some news items get picked up by the financial media, and money gets shifted there. In this case, a lot of money moved into European and Chinese stocks. This was aided by some controversy that the American AI technology was a bust because of Chinese AI. My premise here is that we are at or approaching peak Europe and perhaps peak China shortly. So, if at this point I were contemplating moving money out of the USA, I would decide not do it now. Since I am a firm believer in US markets, the thought would never occur to me. Even so, I think it’s important to evaluate the zeitgeist. My conclusion is that, for the most part, this is a fad. If one is a very adept fast money trader, then China could work for some, and perhaps for a few months. I would say that I would lose money on such an errant venture.

Many Investors are heeding the clarion call of investing in Europe and China

If you are jumping on the bandwagon to Europe now, it is my opinion that you are likely too late to reap significant capital appreciation. Think about what the big attraction for Europe, the first is what has been said about Europe for years, it’s so cheap! The second is the big exciting news that Germany has agreed to raise deficit spending. The last is that Europe, almost as a whole, is committing to spending more on defense. The first is that it’s cheap; I say you get what you pay for. Europe has no start-ups nor any fast-growing medium or large capitalized public companies. Government-led growth is not sustainable, eventually, it leads to malinvestment and inflation. That said, Germany does need to do some deficit investing and building up its armed forces. There are a handful of public European defense companies, and they are likely nearly fully valued.

By comparison, the US is expensive, but you get growth and productivity. It would be great if Europe had real growth; that would mean a much higher business formation rate and conditions to foster faster growth. I haven’t heard any regulation reforms being put forward as yet. There is talk of the idea, but there are a lot of entrenched interests that like things just the way they are. Even if reforms are put through, it would take years to put those reforms in place and see the changes. If the deficit spending is pumped into an economy that doesn’t have growth or more productivity, inflation would be right around the corner. It seems that China has learned this lesson and is now changing its tune (yet again) that they want more growth in Chinese domiciled technology and start-ups. Furthermore, all high-growth businesses no longer have to look over their shoulder. The message to Chinese CEOs and founders will find a more encouraging environment and encouragement. Even Jack Ma has been brought back and shook hands with President Xi recently. Xi is saying all the right things now, but as soon as private enterprise challenges the CCP for power, in my opinion, XI will go back to repression mode.

US stocks are losing investment and trading dollars against a negative narrative that, besides stocks being too expensive, the US economy is losing growth, there is talk of more tariffs, and AI is a bust. I think this set of narratives will dissipate shortly. For one, the big cap stocks have gotten a lot cheaper: Microsoft (MSFT) -16% from its high, NVIDIA (NVDA) -22%, Meta Platforms (META) -12%, Amazon (AMZN) -18%, Alphabet (GOOGL) -16%, and Tesla (TSLA) -46%. I am not listing these because I am interested in buying them all; I am mostly listing them to show that they have already been significantly reduced, which in turn has pressured the overall indexes. When the S&P 500 and the Nasdaq show such a steep drop in such a short while, and right after the S&P 500 hit an all-time high, market commentators cast around for an explanation. Also, there is a very vocal group of money managers that push the notion that foreign markets are a better investment. In any case, the “US economy is done” narrative has gained currency. Let’s look at the AI narrative first.

Everyone knows that DeepSeek, the Chinese AI startup, has the US beat, so AI is dead…

Not so fast, the claims that DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) uses slower chips and is operating super cheaply doesn’t hold water. Not with allegations that Singapore has been selling banned chips into China. The techniques that were used were open-source and were indeed more efficient. All the AI providers are adopting these techniques, so yes, there was innovation there, but not enough to stop the build-out of data centers going on. In fact, by lowering the cost per inference, it lowers the bar for use cases to be cost-effective. Essentially, this will widen the adoption of AI to smaller applications and new services that can be offered to smaller companies. The need for more AI computation will only grow, and the service will be even more valuable. All the hyperscalers have not pulled back on their spending commitments in the 10s of billions. The only negative that has surfaced is coming from MSFT, which has been turning to renting more capacity or renting data centers instead of buying them outright. They still commit to spending $80B, which they have not yet canceled.

Some US stocks that have nothing to do with AI got sold off pretty hard

Just this Friday, Eli Lilly (LLY) lost just under 5% of its value because of what? AI, Europe, or China? No, Friday was just panic selling; that is why my thinking is we are likely at the end of the selling or pretty close. How about Netflix (NFLX)? What does it have to do with a recession, tariffs, or a lower GDP? If a family wants to save money, instead of going out for dinner and a movie, they’ll eat a home-cooked meal and watch Netflix. It is priced low enough that a recession is more likely to accelerate cord-cutting than canceling NFLX. Yet, look at what happened this week. Look at this one-month chart.

Just about 2 weeks ago, NFLX hit its 52-week high, and by this past Friday at its low 858, it lost 20%. There are plenty of stocks like this. Look at Affirm (AFRM); it also set its 52-week high about 2 weeks ago. Here is another 1-month chart.

This comes to an amazing drop of 42% for AFRM, a company that might do better in a slower growth environment because the cost is much lower than a credit card. Here, there is also the fact that Klarna is supposed to go public shortly, so that might also be working on the price. Still, the low of the day was 47.74, and the previous close was 52.64, which is over an 8% drop. There are plenty more examples like this where great stocks fell to ridiculous levels and then bounced; both of these charts have identical bounces. AFRM closed at 51.97 for a loss of only 1.3%, Looking at NFLX, the low on Friday was 858, the previous close was 906 for a loss of 6.3% for the low of the day, yet as we see from the chart, the close was 891 a loss of just 1.7%. I picked these names not randomly because I invested in them on Friday, but I didn’t calculate the bounces, which are very substantial, more than I thought while I was buying them. I think that if you looked at a lot of stocks, the bounce on Friday was noticeable. Does this mean we are at the absolute bottom? No, I have stopped making such ambitious pronouncements. I will say we are bottoming. So let me finish with some reasons why I am optimistic and the jump to Europe and China will lose some of their charm.

Optimism

There are plenty of reasons for optimism, including my belief that the current GDP estimate will move higher and that inflation will cool back down.

This past Friday, Jay Powell, while speaking at the Economic Policy Forum, made some exceedingly dovish statements that seemed to confirm that tax cuts were on the table while rises were decidedly off the table. This was the last day of "Fed Speak" before the blackout period before the next FOMC meeting on March 18-19

The tax bill has gotten through the House, and now the Senate will pass its version, and then they will reconcile the bill. The prospect of having the previous tax cuts either extended or permanent for the next several years will take one large unknown off of the list of unknowns. It is still an unknown, but the House was the first and arguably most difficult step.

The Dollar has dropped substantially against the Euro, and interest rates have been sliding. If these two trends continue, global profits will rise, and US mortgage rates will fall low enough for a rise in refinancing, possibly injecting billions of disposable income to consumers.

Broadcom (AVGO) reported Thursday after the close, and the CEO made some very positive statements about not seeing any slowing for AI spending for their chips. If the AI hyperscalers are buying custom AI chips from AVGO, the AI rally is still on. There will still be tons of demand for NVDA Blackwell chips, too, with many server providers commenting strong orders.

My trades

I had so few trades that I will mention them at the end. I started an investment in NFLX once it fell about 100 points, so my first buy was 950, but I just kept nibbling Thursday and Friday with my lowest purchase at 867. My style of investing is to take small bites, and that is what I did. My cost basis is 917, so if NFLX opens under 917 on Monday, I will buy more. I added a small amount to GOOGL this week, but now I think it will be lower, as the DOJ stated that they still want to break GOOGL up. My cost basis on GOOGL is 168, so I will add if GOOGL does fall below that level. I added to AFRM; it was just too cheap. Although my cost basis in this name was 17, I think the low 50s is an excellent level to add to my investment at this point. I added to Marvell (MRVL) because the stock fell hard on what, I thought, were really good results. I started a position in Robinhood (HOOD). I think they are gaining share, and trading at the mid-40s is a good place to start. If it falls lower, I would be glad to buy more. Ok, so for my trades, I had opened Long Put Options on Hims (HIMS) at the 37 strike and closed it out on Thursday for a decent return. On Friday, I opened Long Put Options on the VIX futures with the 20 strike and the expiration out to May. I had decided, based on the price action I observed and also on historical patterns, that we are likely at a peak for volatility. I would not be surprised to see the VIX well below 20 this week.

Good luck, everyone…
